segments.title: Personifying navies to explain treaty negotiations
segments.summary: Clarke explains why treating navies as characters can reveal the pressures hidden behind formal treaty language.
segments.body: The skit began as a seminar exercise about the informal conversations surrounding historical negotiations. By personifying institutions rather than naming individual officers, Clarke asks learners to consider what each navy needed, feared, and might have said away from the formal conference table.

evidence.note: Clarke describes using the personified-navies skit to help students think through side conversations in treaty negotiations.

segments.title: Five treaty navies meet after Washington
segments.summary: The skit assembles the British, American, Japanese, Italian, and French navies to expose why none regards the treaty as a simple triumph.
segments.body: Clarke stages the five principal Washington Naval Treaty navies in the British Embassy bar after the politicians have left. The comic setup makes a serious point: a settlement presented as a collective success could impose a different strategic and political disappointment on every participant.

evidence.note: The scene introduces the Royal Navy, US Navy, Marine Nationale, Regia Marina, and Imperial Japanese Navy after the treaty.

segments.title: The Royal Navy sees a floor against Treasury cuts
segments.summary: Britain's navy treats treaty limits as protection against deeper peacetime cuts rather than permission to build up to the ceiling.
segments.body: The personified Royal Navy expects the Treasury to reduce defense spending once no immediate enemy can be named. Its consolation is that the treaty ratio might establish a minimum force below which Britain should not fall, showing how arms limits could also serve as an internal budget argument.

evidence.note: The Royal Navy character hopes the treaty will prevent the Treasury from cutting the fleet any further.

segments.title: Paper parity becomes Congress's maximum
segments.summary: The US Navy fears Congress will mistake nominal equality with Britain for a fully funded fleet suited to American distances.
segments.body: In the skit, the American navy argues that a treaty ceiling Britain can use as a minimum will become a maximum in Congress. Formal capital-ship equality does not supply the cruisers it needs, while its existing destroyers are portrayed as too short-ranged for the missions expected of them.

evidence.note: The US Navy character contrasts paper equality with congressional reluctance to fund cruisers and adequate destroyer endurance.

segments.title: Japan's treaty ratio meets army rivalry
segments.summary: The Japanese navy's complaint combines inadequate fleet strength with a violent domestic struggle against the army for policy and resources.
segments.body: Clarke's Imperial Japanese Navy character dismisses British and American budget complaints because its own officers face both spending pressure and political violence. The exchange connects Japan's dissatisfaction with the treaty ratio to interservice rivalry in an island empire that still depended on maritime power.

evidence.note: The Japanese navy character describes army rivalry, threats to officers, budget competition, and too few ships for its tasks.

segments.title: Italy loses the Francesco Caracciolo promise
segments.summary: Italy's comic victory speech mourns the battleship program meant to demonstrate that the Regia Marina and Italian industry had arrived.
segments.body: The Italian navy calls the settlement a glorious but hollow victory because the Francesco Caracciolo-class ambition will not be realized. Clarke uses the boast—and the British and American contributions quietly embedded in the design—to contrast national prestige with the international dependencies behind capital-ship construction.

evidence.note: The Regia Marina character laments the lost Francesco Caracciolo program while the others note foreign machinery and equipment.

segments.title: French parity frustration and the Béarn conversion
segments.summary: France resents being limited alongside Italy after wartime devastation, while salvaging one consolation through an aircraft-carrier conversion.
segments.body: The Marine Nationale character stresses that France emerged from the First World War badly damaged yet received the same capital-ship allocation as Italy. Its new battleships and cruisers are curtailed, but the conversion that became Béarn offers a way to explore carrier aviation using the turbine-powered Normandie-class hull.

evidence.note: The French navy character objects to parity with Italy and identifies an aircraft-carrier conversion as the principal surviving opportunity.

segments.title: Treaty comedy as strategic perspective-taking
segments.summary: Clarke explains how comic institutional voices can test the competing realities beneath the Washington and London naval treaties.
segments.body: Students and academics found the caricatures recognizable because each complaint grows from a real institutional problem. Clarke extended the method to the London treaty system, where the surviving participants' desire to preserve an appearance of peace collided with increasingly obvious military threats.

evidence.note: Clarke reflects on the skit's historical accuracy and describes applying the same method to the London naval treaties.

segments.title: Historical teaching through options and context
segments.summary: A closing reflection frames teaching as helping learners see credible alternatives rather than prescribing a single route.
segments.body: Clarke describes a lecturer's role as checking in, signposting help, and widening the set of options a student can see. The same habit underpins the treaty skit: step back from the official outcome, identify pressures on each actor, and consider routes that are obscured when attention narrows to one immediate problem.

evidence.note: Clarke explains his approach to teaching by presenting options, context, and support while leaving decisions to adult learners.
